profile.name: Pardhu Sri Rushi Varma Konduru
profile.title: Cybersecurity & ML Security Researcher
profile.subtitle: Runtime-Governed Intelligence Systems
profile.bio: Exploring adversarial AI, systems security, and runtime-governed architectures. Building modular, reversible, and compositionally safe intelligence systems for adversarial resilience.

about.title: Research Philosophy
paragraphs: I am a cybersecurity-oriented systems and machine learning researcher interested in the foundations of autonomous intelligence.

My work is driven by a core concern: how to design adaptive systems that remain structurally accountable. I am particularly interested in reinforcement learning, runtime control, and the intersection of security principles with machine intelligence.

I approach intelligence as a governed process rather than a static artifact. This perspective leads me to think in terms of modularity, authority separation, invariant enforcement, and reversibility. I am drawn to architectures where behavior can evolve without compromising oversight, and where autonomy is bounded by explicit control mechanisms.

My background in cybersecurity strongly shapes how I think about AI systems — prioritizing failure modes, attack surfaces, lifecycle control, and containment before capability. I am motivated by building systems that are not only intelligent, but architecturally disciplined.

In the long term, I aim to contribute to research at the intersection of secure systems and adaptive intelligence, where formal rigor and real-world constraints shape the future of autonomous technologies.

researchAreas.title: Reversible Behavioral Learning
researchAreas.subtitle: Structural Identity Preservation
researchAreas.description: Investigating parameter isolation techniques that enable deterministic rollback without model identity corruption or capability degradation.

researchAreas.title: Adversarial AI Security
researchAreas.subtitle: Red-Team ML & Robustness
researchAreas.description: Exploring attack-defense dynamics in intelligent systems through adversarial stress-testing, poisoning attacks, and evasion techniques.

researchAreas.title: Runtime Governance
researchAreas.subtitle: Safe AI Control Mechanisms
researchAreas.description: Designing kill-switches, constraint layers, and runtime safety systems for high-stakes autonomous agents and swarm intelligence.

researchAreas.title: Agentic Swarm Intelligence
researchAreas.subtitle: Distributed Defense Systems
researchAreas.description: Autonomous multi-agent coordination for cyber defense with role-switching, emergent behavior, and collective decision-making.
researchAreas.keywords: swarm agents • distributed systems • autonomous defense

researchAreas.title: RL-LoRA Behavior Systems
researchAreas.subtitle: Modular Skill Composition
researchAreas.description: Dynamic behavioral patching through low-rank adaptation modules that enable compositional skill assembly and runtime reconfiguration.
researchAreas.keywords: low-rank adaptation • behavioral modules • skill composition

researchAreas.title: Cyber-Physical Security
researchAreas.subtitle: CPS Threat Modeling
researchAreas.description: Secure sensing, anomaly detection, and adversarial resilience in physical-world AI-enabled systems and cyber-physical infrastructures.
researchAreas.keywords: cps security • threat modeling • physical adversaries

publications.title: On the Structural Limitations of Weight-Based Neural Adaptation and the Role of Reversible Behavioral Learning
publications.metadata: Preprint • arXiv: arXiv:2603.02934 • DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.18761938, 10.48550/arXiv.2603.02934 • 2026 • AI Safety / Machine Learning
publications.abstract: Modern neural models are typically adapted by updating parameters through fine-tuning or alignment. While effective for task performance, such updates can degrade reasoning, overwrite capabilities, or alter functional identity. We examine this as structural irreversibility and propose reversible adaptation frameworks that isolate learned behaviors from core parameters, enabling deterministic rollback with measurable recoverability guarantees.

systems.name: AADS
systems.description: Agentic AI Defense Swarms with safe governance and swarm-level autonomy. Multi-agent system designed for autonomous cyber defense operations with role-switching capabilities, emergent coordination, and runtime safety constraints. Built with distributed architecture and fault-tolerant infrastructure.

systems.name: RLAE (REVA4)
systems.description: Runtime-Governed Intelligence Systems for modular, reversible, and compositionally safe behaviors. Enables dynamic loading of behavioral LoRA patches with deterministic rollback capabilities. Implements behavioral governance and runtime safety constraints for adversarial resilience.

systems.name: GNIM
systems.description: Geospatial Network Imaging & Mapping: A software-defined spatial intelligence framework for coordinate-aware network observability. Fuses logical network behavior with physical deployment context, enabling dynamic visualization of traffic patterns, anomaly density, and zone-level activity.

systems.name: OpenLoRA
systems.description: Open-source framework for streamlined LLM fine-tuning using Low-Rank Adaptation. Transforms local environments into intelligent, self-adaptive LoRA training infrastructure with Prometheus monitoring and Grafana visualization. Features dataset synthesis and evaluation engines.

systems.name: Project Ouroboros
systems.description: Modular ESP32-based cybersecurity platform for wireless defense, monitoring, and controlled research. Hardware and firmware modules for cyber-physical security experimentation, featuring embedded systems integration and real-time RF threat response capabilities.

contact.collaborationText: Open to research collaboration, academic discussion, and systems-oriented AI security dialogue. Aspiring toward graduate research at CISPA, Max Planck, or Helmholtz centers. Interested in AI safety, adversarial resilience, and runtime-governed systems.
contact.footerNote: Available for research collaboration and academic partnerships.
